questions.specification: What is a defining feature of autotrophs?

labeledSubSubTopics.path: C - Interaction and Interdependence > C4.2 Transfer of energy and matter > C4.2.6 Autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.title: C4.2.6 Autotrophs
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks for the defining feature of autotrophs; this matches the topic explicitly titled 'Autotrophs' within ecosystem energy/matter transfer.

options.content: They consume other organisms for food
options.markscheme: Autotrophs are producers; organisms that consume others for organic nutrients are heterotrophs.

options.content: They fix carbon using external energy sources
options.markscheme: Autotrophs synthesize organic molecules from inorganic carbon (e.g. CO$_2$) using energy from light (photoautotrophs) or chemical reactions (chemoautotrophs).

options.content: They obtain energy by consuming organic compounds but do not fix carbon
options.markscheme: This describes heterotrophs, which rely on organic carbon sources rather than fixing inorganic carbon.

options.content: They fix carbon but use organic compounds as their energy source
options.markscheme: Fixing carbon while using organic compounds for energy is not a defining feature of autotrophs; autotrophs use light or inorganic chemical energy to fix carbon. Using organic energy sources is characteristic of heterotrophic nutrition.

options.content: Arrow 1, occurring in aerobic forest soils
options.markscheme: Methanogenesis by methanogenic archaea requires anaerobic conditions; aerobic forest soils do not support this process.

options.content: Arrow 2, occurring in anaerobic waterlogged sediments
options.markscheme: Arrow 2 shows conversion of organic matter to methane; methanogenic archaea carry out methanogenesis in anaerobic, waterlogged environments (e.g., sediments, wetlands).

options.content: Arrow 3, occurring through the reduction of methane in the stratosphere
options.markscheme: Arrow 3 represents methane being oxidized to $CO_2$ (not reduced), and this is not methanogenesis by archaea nor restricted to the stratosphere.

options.content: Arrow 4, occurring during the formation of limestone in the ocean
options.markscheme: Arrow 4 represents carbon transfer from fossil fuels to atmospheric $CO_2$ (combustion); limestone formation is unrelated to methanogenic archaea and does not produce methane.

options.markscheme: X represents the movement of inorganic CO$$_2$$ from the atmosphere into the biomass of producers, which is photosynthesis. Y represents the release of CO$$_2$$ back into the atmosphere by consumers during metabolic activity, which is cell respiration.
Respiration moves carbon from biomass to the atmosphere, which is the opposite direction of arrow X.
Feeding involves the transfer of organic carbon from producers to consumers, which is the horizontal arrow in the diagram, not arrow Y.
Combustion involves the burning of biomass or fossil fuels, which is not the primary process by which consumers return carbon to the atmosphere.

- Describes that other forest/woody biomes (e.g. OF, SW) show intermediate responses <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Notes that grasslands (GRA) and/or croplands (CRO) show lower responses than EBF <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Notes that $C_4$ systems show the smallest response, consistent with reduced limitation by $CO_2$ compared with $C_3$ photosynthesis <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Based on Image C, explain how declining plant nutrient content could reduce secondary production in food webs.
parts.markscheme:
- States that concentrations of several essential nutrients/minerals decrease at elevated $CO_2$ (e.g. N, P, Fe, Zn), lowering food quality for consumers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that reduced N (and other nutrients) can reduce protein synthesis in herbivores, slowing growth and reducing biomass gain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that consumers may need to eat more plant biomass to obtain sufficient nutrients, increasing losses via respiration/egestion and reducing net biomass production <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links reduced herbivore growth/reproduction to less food/biomass available for higher trophic levels (lower secondary production) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest how this might affect trophic efficiency and biomass transfer in ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggests trophic efficiency would decrease because a smaller proportion of ingested plant material is converted into new consumer biomass when nutrients are limiting <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggests more plant material is lost as faeces/undigested material due to low nutrient content, reducing biomass transfer <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Suggests more energy is used for maintenance/respiration as consumers process extra food to meet nutrient needs, leaving less for growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Predicts reduced biomass/abundance at higher trophic levels due to diminished transfer up the food chain <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why the $CO_2$ fertilization effect, although increasing GPP, might contribute to positive feedback in the climate system over time. Refer to at least two of Images A–C.
parts.markscheme:
- Uses Image A and/or B to explain that increased $CO_2$ can increase GPP (positive fertilization response) but the response is not uniform across regions/biomes <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses Image B to note that some systems (e.g. $C_4$) show a small response, limiting additional carbon uptake at ecosystem/global scales <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Uses Image C to explain that nutrient dilution/declines (e.g. N, Fe, Zn) can lead to nutrient limitation that constrains sustained biomass accumulation and carbon sequestration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that if carbon uptake saturates/declines while respiration and decomposition continue, net ecosystem carbon storage may not increase and atmospheric $CO_2$ can keep rising <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explains that increased biomass can increase fuel loads, so drought/heat can increase fire frequency/intensity, releasing stored carbon (positive feedback) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Describe one scenario in which increased plant growth might shift to a net carbon source.
parts.markscheme:
- Describes an environmental stress scenario (e.g. drought/heatwave) that reduces photosynthesis/GPP despite elevated $CO_2$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes that respiration and/or decomposition can remain high (or increase with temperature), so $R > GPP$ and the system becomes a net $CO_2$ source <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Describes a disturbance scenario such as wildfire, pest outbreak, or land-use change that rapidly releases carbon from biomass/soil, outweighing gains from increased growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Links reduced nutrient availability (consistent with Image C) to limited long-term carbon storage and greater turnover of biomass to the atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Any other correct, clearly described scenario in which carbon outputs (respiration, decomposition, combustion) exceed carbon inputs (GPP), making net ecosystem production negative <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.content: Energy is recycled by saprotrophic bacteria and returned to the producers.

options.content: Most energy is dissipated as heat during metabolic processes.
options.markscheme: In an ecosystem, energy is lost at each trophic level (typically around 90%) primarily as heat resulting from cellular respiration. This heat cannot be converted back into chemical energy and leaves the ecosystem, ensuring that energy pyramids are always upright.

options.content: The biomass of primary consumers is greater than the biomass of producers.
options.markscheme: This refers to an inverted pyramid of biomass, which can occasionally occur in aquatic systems, but it does not explain why energy is lost. Regardless of biomass, energy content always decreases at higher levels due to the laws of thermodynamics.

options.content: Higher trophic levels have more individuals, spreading total energy thinly.
options.markscheme: This is generally false; typically, both the number of individuals and the total biomass decrease at higher trophic levels because there is less energy available to support them.

options.content: The density of the gas relative to the surrounding air
options.markscheme: The density of a gas determines its buoyancy or layering in the atmosphere, but it does not dictate its ability to trap heat or contribute to the greenhouse effect.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: This topic specifically highlights that both autotrophs and heterotrophs perform cell respiration to release energy, which is the specific knowledge required to correctly identify that processes X and Y (carbon moving from both groups to the atmosphere) represent respiration.

options.markscheme: W is photosynthesis (carbon fixation) and X is respiration in autotrophs; only X represents respiration.

In the carbon cycle, both autotrophs (producers) and heterotrophs (consumers) perform cell respiration. This process releases carbon dioxide ($CO_{2}$) as a byproduct, moving carbon from the organisms back into the atmosphere. Therefore, both process X (autotrophs to atmosphere) and process Y (heterotrophs to atmosphere) represent respiration. Process W represents photosynthesis (carbon fixation) and process Z represents feeding (carbon transfer through the food chain).
Y is respiration in heterotrophs, but Z is feeding (transfer of carbon in organic molecules), not respiration.
W is photosynthesis and Z is feeding; neither represents respiration.

parts.content: Calculate the efficiency of energy transfer between primary consumers and secondary consumers. Show your working.
parts.markscheme:
Energy transfer efficiency is calculated using the formula:

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Energy at higher trophic level}}{\text{Energy at lower trophic level}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{1\,000}{10\,000} \times 100 = 10\%$$

Final Answer: 10% Efficiency

<marks>1 mark</marks> for correct substitution
<marks>1 mark</marks> for correct percentage calculation

parts.content: State two reasons why energy transfer between trophic levels is inefficient.
parts.markscheme:
Any two of the following:

*   **Heat Loss** – Energy is lost as heat through respiration, metabolism, and movement/activity of organisms <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Incomplete Consumption** – Some parts of organisms (e.g., bones, cellulose) are not eaten <marks>1 mark</marks>
*   **Excretion** – Energy is lost through waste products like feces and urine <marks>1 mark</marks>

Award <marks>1 mark</marks> for each correct reason up to <marks>2 marks</marks>.

options.markscheme: Incorrect. The fundamental niche is the total range of environmental conditions under which a species can survive and reproduce, represented here by the dashed line.
Correct. The realized niche is the actual conditions and resources a species uses after taking into account biotic interactions like competition and predation.
Incorrect. Physiological tolerance limits are typically associated with the fundamental niche (dashed line), not the distribution restricted by competition.
Incorrect. Competitive exclusion is a principle stating that two species cannot occupy the same niche indefinitely; it does not describe a specific curve on a performance graph.

parts.content: Define biomass and explain how it is measured.
parts.markscheme:
- **Define** biomass as the total mass of living organisms in a given area or volume (often expressed as dry mass per unit area/volume, e.g. $\text{kg m}^{-2}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that biomass is measured by sampling organisms from a known area/volume (e.g. using quadrats/plots), drying to a constant mass to remove water, then weighing to obtain dry mass and expressing this per unit area/volume <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Compare the biomass of a tropical rainforest and a desert ecosystem.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the biomass of the tropical rainforest ($45.0\ \text{kg m}^{-2}$) is higher than that of the desert ($0.7\ \text{kg m}^{-2}$) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Calculate/compare** that this is about $\frac{45.0}{0.7}\approx 64$ times higher (accept “much higher” with correct data reference) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that rainforests have dense vegetation supported by high rainfall and warm temperatures, leading to high biomass accumulation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that deserts have sparse vegetation due to limited water availability and temperature stress, resulting in low biomass (any one relevant limitation) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Explain why primary production is higher in rainforests compared to deserts.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** that rainforests receive high rainfall so water is not a limiting factor for photosynthesis and growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that higher temperatures in rainforests increase enzyme-controlled reactions (up to an optimum), supporting faster photosynthesis and growth <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Explain** that high, year-round light availability/long growing season in rainforests increases photosynthetic time and total carbon fixation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **State** that greater leaf area/vertical stratification (more canopy layers) increases light interception and total productivity <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Suggest reasons why biomass accumulation may differ in different ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- **Suggest** that differences in abiotic factors (e.g. temperature, precipitation, light) limit photosynthesis and growth rates of primary producers <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Suggest** that nutrient availability/soil fertility affects plant growth and thus biomass accumulation <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Suggest** that rates of decomposition/respiration/herbivory or disturbance (e.g. fire, grazing) can reduce standing biomass even if NPP is high <marks>1 mark</marks>
- **Suggest** that human impacts (e.g. deforestation, land management) can restrict or enhance the accumulation of organic matter <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Biomass
parts.markscheme:
Biomass is the total mass of living matter within a given unit of environmental area.
<marks>2 marks</marks>

steps.title: Identify the objective
steps.content: In ecology, we often need to measure how much biological material is present in an ecosystem to understand energy flow. This question asks for a formal definition of **biomass**, which is a key concept in **Topic C4.2 (Transfer of energy and matter)**.

steps.title: Quantify organic matter
steps.content: The first part of the definition focuses on what is being measured. Biomass represents the **total mass** of all living or organic matter. In many studies, this is specifically measured as **dry mass** to exclude water weight, which can fluctuate and doesn't contain stored chemical energy.

steps.title: Define the spatial boundary
steps.content:
To make the measurement meaningful, it must be localized. We define biomass within a specific **ecosystem** or a **unit of area** (for terrestrial ecosystems) or **volume** (for aquatic ecosystems).

Common units include:
*   $g \cdot m^{-2}$ (grams per square meter)
*   $kg \cdot ha^{-1}$ (kilograms per hectare)

steps.title: State the formal definition
steps.content:
Combining these two components gives us the complete definition required for full marks:

**Biomass is the total mass of living matter within a given unit of environmental area.**

highlights.text: Biomass is the total mass of living matter within a given unit of environmental area.

steps.title: Understanding the marks
steps.content:
To earn the **2 marks** available for this definition, your answer must include:
1.  The concept of **total mass** of living/organic matter.
2.  The context of a **given area** or ecosystem.

steps.title: Identify the task
steps.content: To earn full marks for this definition, we need to describe what biomass is and how it is measured within an ecosystem. In **Topic C4.2: Transfer of energy and matter**, biomass is key to understanding how much energy is available to be passed along a food chain.

steps.title: Focus on dry mass
steps.content: From a measurement perspective, scientists define biomass as the **dry mass** of organic material. We exclude water because the water content in organisms fluctuates significantly and does not contain stored chemical energy. By measuring only the dry organic matter, we get a consistent value of the biological tissue present.

steps.title: Define the spatial scale
steps.content:
Biomass is always measured over a specific region. It is expressed as the mass of organic matter per **unit area** (for terrestrial ecosystems) or **unit volume** (for aquatic ecosystems).

Common units include:
- Grams per square meter ($g \, m^{-2}$)
- Tonnes per square kilometer ($t \, km^{-2}$)

steps.title: Connect to chemical energy
steps.content: The biological importance of biomass is that it represents the **chemical energy** stored in the carbon compounds of the organisms. This energy is what becomes available to the next trophic level when an organism is consumed.

steps.title: State the final definition
steps.content:
Combining these points for your exam answer:

**Biomass** is the total dry mass of organic matter in living organisms (or a specific trophic level) within a given unit of environmental area at a particular time.

highlights.text: Biomass is the total mass of living matter within a given unit of environmental area.

parts.content: Gross primary production
parts.markscheme:
Gross primary production is the total amount of energy captured by photosynthesis in a given area and time period, before any is used for respiration.
<marks>2 marks</marks>

steps.title: Identify the biological process
steps.content: To define Gross Primary Production (GPP), we first look at the role of producers (autotrophs). In an ecosystem, these organisms capture solar energy and convert it into chemical energy through the process of **photosynthesis**. This is the fundamental entry point for energy into almost all food chains, as explored in **Topic C4.2: Transfer of energy and matter**.

steps.title: Define energy capture
steps.content:
Gross Primary Production represents the **total** rate at which this energy conversion happens. It is the total amount of light energy that is converted to chemical energy (in the form of organic molecules like glucose) by the producers in an ecosystem.

To be scientifically precise, we must measure this over a specific **area** and **time period** (for example, $kJ \cdot m^{-2} \cdot yr^{-1}$).

highlights.text: total amount of energy captured by photosynthesis
in a given area and time period

steps.title: Distinguish from net production
steps.content:
The term "Gross" is critical here. It refers to the energy captured **before** the plants use any of it for their own metabolic needs. Since plants are living organisms, they must perform **cell respiration** to stay alive, which consumes some of the energy they just produced.

GPP is the "paycheck" before taxes (respiration) are taken out. What remains after respiration is called Net Primary Production (NPP).

highlights.text: before any is used for respiration

steps.title: Final Definition
steps.content:
Based on the markscheme requirements, the full definition is:

**Gross Primary Production (GPP)** is the total amount of energy captured by photosynthesis in a given area and time period, before any is used for respiration.

steps.title: Identify the energy flow
steps.content: In an ecosystem, energy enters through autotrophs (like plants) that capture sunlight. This process is covered in **Topic C4.2: Transfer of energy and matter**. To define Gross Primary Production (GPP), we look at the total amount of chemical energy these organisms create before they use any for themselves.

steps.title: The energy budget equation
steps.content:
The energy budget of a producer can be expressed by a simple relationship. The total energy captured ($GPP$) is divided into two parts: the energy stored as new biomass ($NPP$) and the energy lost through the plant's own metabolic processes ($R$).

$$\text{GPP} = \text{NPP} + R$$

steps.title: Define GPP as a sum
steps.content:
Using the energy budget approach, we define **Gross Primary Production** as the sum of the **Net Primary Production (NPP)** and **autotrophic respiration (R)**. 

This represents the "gross" or total amount of solar energy that has been fixed into chemical energy (glucose) by photosynthesis in a specific area over a specific time.

steps.title: Final definition for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks, ensure your definition includes the key components of the energy budget:

1. It is the **total energy captured** by photosynthesis.
2. It occurs within a **given area and time period**.
3. It is the energy available **before** any is used for respiration.

In formula terms: $$GPP = NPP + R$$

parts.content: Identify two components of net primary production (NPP) in mangrove ecosystems and calculate their combined total in $\text{Tg C year}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Identifies one valid NPP component from the figure with correct value (e.g. litterfall production 69; tree growth as wood 59; root production 44; dead wood accumulation 46) <marks>1 mark</marks>

_**OR**_

- Identifies a second valid NPP component from the figure with correct value (as above) <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Calculates the correct combined total in $\text{Tg C year}^{-1}$ for the two selected components (e.g. 69+59=128; 69+44=113; 59+44=103; 46+69=115; 46+59=105; 46+44=90) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award max 2 marks total. Accept any two valid NPP components from the figure and their correct sum.</note>

_**OR**_

<note>Award max 2 marks total. Accept any two valid loss pathways shown in the figure provided the sum is correct.</note>

parts.content: Explain why secondary productivity is significantly lower than primary productivity in mangrove ecosystems.
parts.markscheme:
- Energy is lost as heat through respiration at each trophic level, reducing energy available for growth/biomass <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Not all primary producer biomass is consumed by herbivores (e.g. much plant material remains as litter/wood/roots) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Of the biomass that is ingested, not all is assimilated; some is lost as faeces/egestion/excretion <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Energy is transferred to decomposers/detrital pathways rather than to herbivore biomass (so secondary production remains low) <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award max 2 marks total.</note>

parts.content: Explain how mangroves act as carbon sinks and why their ecosystem respiration (ER) relative to gross primary productivity (GPP) supports this role.
parts.markscheme:
- GPP exceeds ER (ER/GPP $<1$), so more $\text{CO}_2$ is fixed by photosynthesis than released by respiration <marks>1 mark</marks>
- The difference between GPP and ER is net ecosystem production (positive NEP), leading to net carbon gain <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Net carbon gain is stored/sequestered in biomass (wood/roots/litter/dead wood) and/or in soils (soil accumulation) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Some carbon is retained long term because soil/wood decomposition can be slow (e.g. anoxic/waterlogged conditions), supporting sink function <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award max 2 marks total.</note>

parts.content: Discuss how the combustion of biomass (e.g. dead mangrove wood) or fossil fuels releases stored carbon back into the atmosphere, linking this to the long-term role of mangroves as carbon sinks.
parts.markscheme:
- Combustion oxidizes carbon compounds, releasing stored carbon mainly as $\text{CO}_2$ to the atmosphere <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Burning biomass returns carbon that was previously stored in plant tissues (including dead wood), reducing the amount held in the ecosystem <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Burning fossil fuels releases carbon stored over geological timescales, increasing atmospheric $\text{CO}_2$ and counteracting natural sinks <marks>1 mark</marks>
- If mangroves are degraded/cleared and biomass/soil carbon is burned or exposed to oxidation, long-term sequestration is reduced and the sink can become a source <marks>1 mark</marks>

<note>Award max 2 marks total.</note>
